notable.notable_blurb: Marine Captain, VMF-214 Black Sheep Squadron, WWII
notable.primary_url_label: Military Times Valor — Distinguished Flying Cross citation

career.notes: Enlisted June 24, 1941. Commissioned July 17, 1942. Marine pilot, NAS Jacksonville instructor. Joined VMF-214 summer 1943. Division leader.

military_service.notes: Black Sheep Squadron. Solomon Islands campaign, two tours. Shot down January 3, 1944 Rabaul. BTL064.

cross_connections.display_label: Grand-uncle; senator's grandson; died Los Angeles, 1930
fell in the same Pacific war, killed on Negros 1945

narrative_blocks.header: Died over Rabaul; Boyington captured the same day
narrative_blocks.body: Captain George Malcolm Ashmun was shot down over Rabaul on January 3, 1944, flying with VMF-214 — the Black Sheep Squadron — when his Corsair burst into flames and crashed into the ocean. His wingman, Major Gregory 'Pappy' Boyington, was himself shot down minutes later and spent the rest of the war as a Japanese prisoner of war.

narrative_blocks.header: First kill; last flight over Rabaul, 1944
narrative_blocks.body: George Ashmun flew 67 combat missions over two tours with the Black Sheep without shooting down a single enemy aircraft — nearly 150 combat hours, scoreless. On January 3, 1944, flying as Boyington's wingman on a sweep of Rabaul, he finally scored his first aerial victory — and was killed in the same engagement.

narrative_blocks.header: Boyington's wingman on his squadron's last combat day
narrative_blocks.body: Boyington chose Ashmun as his wingman knowing he was eager to get on the scoreboard. Ashmun had told him: 'Go ahead and shoot all you want, Gramps. All I'll do is keep them off your tail.' As Ashmun's plane trailed smoke toward the water, Boyington screamed at him to dive — but George's plane burst into flames before he could answer.

narrative_blocks.header: Crewmates mourned him as 'a real nice guy'
narrative_blocks.body: Fellow VMF-211 pilot Wallace Thompson said that 'a real shock wave went through the pilots' camp' at the loss of Ashmun and Boyington. Ed Olander, Ashmun's closest companion — who had trained with him in Jacksonville, crossed the country with him on the Santa Fe Chief, and bunked with him in the Pacific — said George's death troubled him more than any other loss in the war.

On January 3, 1944, flying as Boyington's wingman on a sweep of Rabaul, he finally scored his first aerial victory — and was killed in the same engagement.

All I'll do is keep them off your tail.' As Ashmun's plane trailed smoke toward the water, Boyington screamed at him to dive — but George's plane burst into flames before he could answer.

narrative_blocks.header: Crewmates mourned him as 'a real nice guy'
narrative_blocks.body: Fellow VMF-211 pilot Wallace Thompson said that 'a real shock wave went through the pilots' camp' at the loss of Ashmun and Boyington. Ed Olander, Ashmun's closest companion — who had trained with him in Jacksonville, crossed the country with him on the Santa Fe Chief, and bunked with him in the Pacific — said George's death troubled him more than any other loss in the war.

Charles Ashmun Sr. died in December 1931 at forty-five years old — before either of his sons entered military service in World War II. His son Charles Jr. served in the U.S. Navy 1942-1945, and his son Captain George Malcolm Ashmun flew with VMF-214 and was killed over Rabaul on January 3, 1944.

George Ashmun was born in 1849, three years before his father Lewis Ashmun died aboard the USS Decatur off Nicaragua in 1852 — he never knew his father. His grandfather was U.S. Senator Eli Porter Ashmun of Massachusetts; his uncle was Congressman George Ashmun who presided over Lincoln's 1860 convention.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became a Marine pilot
narrative_blocks.body: George Ashmun's son Charles and daughter-in-law Mary Belcher Young raised two sons in Newark, New Jersey — one of whom, Captain George Malcolm Ashmun, flew with VMF-214, the Black Sheep Squadron, and was killed over Rabaul in 1944. His grandson carried the Ashmun name from New Jersey to the Pacific theater.

burialCemetery.name: Manila American Cemetery and Memorial
burialCemetery.notes: Manila, Philippines. American Battle Monuments Commission. Contains HD4955 Capt. George Malcolm Ashmun on Tablets of the Missing — United States Marine Corps, Missing In Action. He has no known grave; his name appears on the Tablets of the Missing.
